Biography

I have been a faculty member at Texas A&M University since 1995, contributing to its

academic and creative communities for nearly three decades. During this time, I have held

positions as a Professor in the Departments of Visualization and Architecture and am

currently a Professor in the Visual Computing and Computational Media Section of the

newly established School of Performance, Visualization, and Fine Arts. I also hold a joint

appointment with the Department of Computer Science and Engineering, reflecting the

transdisciplinary nature of my teaching and research.

I received my Ph.D. in Electrical and Computer Engineering from the Georgia Institute of

Technology in 1992, where my academic foundation in engineering laid the groundwork for

my diverse research interests, which span computer graphics, geometric modeling, and

topology. My work focuses on developing innovative computational tools and methodologies

that blend artistic creativity with technical rigor, such as my contributions to topological mesh

modeling and the creation of the TopMod system, widely used by artists and educators.

Beyond academia, I am a professional cartoonist, illustrator, and caricaturist, having

published over 750 works in various formats. My artistic endeavors extend to children's

books, popular science articles, and a bi-monthly column in the flagship magazine of the

IEEE Computer Society, IEEE Computer. My unique integration of artistic intuition with

computational techniques has allowed me to mentor nearly 100 graduate students, many of

published over 750 works in various formats. My artistic endeavors extend to children's

books, popular science articles, and a bi-monthly column in the flagship magazine of the

IEEE Computer Society, IEEE Computer. My unique integration of artistic intuition with

computational techniques has allowed me to mentor nearly 100 graduate students, many of

whom now hold leadership roles in companies such as PIXAR, Disney, DreamWorks, and

Google.

My ongoing goal is to bridge the disciplines of computation and creativity, advancing fields

like interactive storytelling, computational aesthetics, and design visualization while

continuing to inspire and support the next generation of innovators.

Catalog Description

Computer Aided Sculpting. (2-3). Credit 3. Mathematical and artistic principles of 3-

D modeling and sculpting; includes proportions, skeletal foundation, expression

and posture, line of action; curves, surfaces and volumes, interpolation and

approximation, parametric and rational parametric polynomials, constructive solid

geometry, and implicit representations. Prerequisite: Approval of instructor. Cross-

listed with VIZA 657.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Approval of instructor. Cross Listing:

VIZA 657/CSCE 648.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Apply mathematical foundations of computer-aided sculpting,

including curves, surfaces, volumes, interpolation, approximation,

and parametric and rational parametric representations.

Analyze and construct geometric models using advanced

representation schemes such as subdivision surfaces, implicit

surfaces, constructive solid geometry, and Voronoi-based

structures.

Understand and apply topological concepts in geometric

modeling, including  3-manifold meshes , connectivity, and

topological constraints relevant to sculpting and shape design.

Apply principles from  Morse theory  to reason about shape

structure, critical points, and topological changes in surface and

volumetric models.

Integrate artistic principles—such as proportion, skeletal

structure, posture, expression, and line of action—with rigorous

mathematical and computational modeling methods.

Design and implement computational sculpting workflows and

algorithms that balance mathematical correctness, topological

consistency, and artistic intent.

Evaluate modeling techniques and representations in terms of

robustness, expressiveness, and suitability for visualization,

animation, or fabrication applications.

Special Course Designation

None

This course is a  (C) cross-listed  course. It is a  prescribed elective  for

algorithms that balance mathematical correctness, topological

consistency, and artistic intent.

Evaluate modeling techniques and representations in terms of

robustness, expressiveness, and suitability for visualization,

animation, or fabrication applications.

Special Course Designation

None

This course is a  (C) cross-listed  course. It is a  prescribed elective  for

the  PhD in Visual Computing & Interactive Media  program.

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Recommended

Conference Articles on Shape Modeling

Notes:

Papers are published in conferences such as ACM SIGGRAPH, Solid

and Physical Modeling, Shape Modeling International, Geometric

Modeling and Processing, SIAM Geometric Design, Symposium of

Geometry Processing, Symposium of Computational Fabrication,

Advanced Architectural Geometry. Eurographics, and Graphics

Interface. You can search for them in Google Scholar and access them

through the Texas A&M University Library.

This material Is: Recommended

Shape Modeling Articles from Computer Aided Design and Engineering & Computer

Graphics Journals

Notes:

Now ,  most   conference   papers   on   Shape   Modeling   are   published   in

journals   such   as   ACM   TOG ,  IEEE   TVCG ,  Computer   Graphics   Forum ,

Computer   Aided   Design ,  Computers  &  Graphics ,  IEEE   Computer

Graphics   and   Applications .  Moreover ,  Engineering   and   Science

journals ,  such   as   PNAS   or   Advanced   Manufacturing ,  also   publish

Shape   Modeling   papers .  You   can   search   for   them   in   Google   Scholar

and   access   them   through   the   Texas   A & M   University   Library .

This material Is: Recommended

Books on Shape Modeling

Notes:

There are many course notes and books on Shape Modeling. Most of

them are useful for the class.

Grading Policy

Grading Breakdown

Quizzes:  52 points

Projects:  36 points

Class Participation:  12 points

The highest possible total is  100 points .

Grading Scale:

A:  Grade > 90 points

B:  80 < Grade  ≤  90 points

C:  70 < Grade  ≤  80 points

D:  60 < Grade  ≤  70 points

F:  Grade  ≤  60 points

Quizzes:

We will have weekly quizzes, which will not necessarily be equally

weighted. Quizzes will be given and graded using WebAssign. Make

sure to pay attention to the due dates and times. If you miss the

deadline, your quiz will be graded at  50% of the actual score .

Projects:

Students will be responsible for completing six projects. The projects

will be discussed in the class. To be graded, all required materials

should be uploaded to the class website and the designated Google

Drive folder on or before the deadline. There are six required projects,

deadline, your quiz will be graded at  50% of the actual score .

Projects:

Students will be responsible for completing six projects. The projects

will be discussed in the class. To be graded, all required materials

should be uploaded to the class website and the designated Google

Drive folder on or before the deadline. There are six required projects,

each equally weighted, contributing 1/6  (6 points out of 36)  to the

final grade (out of 100). Grading will be based on both quality and

process. If you miss the deadline, your project will be graded at  50% of

the actual score .

Class Participation:

Class participation will be determined mainly by attendance. Students

will lose  1.0 point  from their class participation grade for each class

they miss, up to a maximum deduction of  12 points . Students who

regularly contribute insightful comments during class discussions may

receive bonus points in class

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Quizzes:  If you miss the deadline, your quiz will be graded at  50% of the actual score .

Projects:  If you miss the deadline, your project will be graded at  50% of the actual

score .

Course Schedule

Week 1 — Course Overview and Shape as Structure

Computer-aided sculpting as a mathematical and artistic

discipline

Artistic foundations: proportion, skeletal structure, posture,

expression, line of action

Shape representations as abstractions of form

Part I: Shape Representations (Overview First)

Week 2 — Curve-Based Representations

Curves as building blocks of shape

Polynomial, parametric, and rational parametric curves

Interpolation vs. approximation

Week 3 — Surface-Based Representations

Parametric and rational parametric surfaces

Tensor-product surfaces and surface continuity

Surfaces as sculpting primitives

Week 4 — Mesh-Based Representations

Polygonal and triangular meshes

Connectivity, adjacency, and validity

Introduction to  3-manifold meshes

Week 5 — Volumetric and Implicit Representations

Volumetric representations

Implicit surfaces and scalar fields

Blending and volumetric sculpting

Week 6 — Constructive and Spatial Decompositions

Constructive Solid Geometry (CSG)

Voronoi and Delaunay structures

Hybrid representations

Part II: Refinement and Structural Theory

Week 7 — Subdivision Surfaces

Subdivision schemes

Limit surfaces and smoothness

Subdivision as refinement, not representation

Week 8 — Topology of Sculpted Shapes

Topological invariants

Blending and volumetric sculpting

Week 6 — Constructive and Spatial Decompositions

Constructive Solid Geometry (CSG)

Voronoi and Delaunay structures

Hybrid representations

Part II: Refinement and Structural Theory

Week 7 — Subdivision Surfaces

Subdivision schemes

Limit surfaces and smoothness

Subdivision as refinement, not representation

Week 8 — Topology of Sculpted Shapes

Topological invariants

Genus, handles, tunnels, cavities

Topology-preserving vs. topology-changing operations

Week 9 — Morse Theory and Shape Analysis

Morse functions and critical points

Scalar fields on surfaces and volumes

Shape decomposition and structural reasoning

Part III: Algorithms and Sculpting Workflows

Week 10 — Algorithmic Operations on Shapes

Local vs. global sculpting operations

Topological edits and constraints

Structure-aware modeling

Week 11 — Representation Conversion and Hybrid Modeling

Conversions between curves, surfaces, meshes, and implicits

Robustness and numerical issues

Choosing representations based on intent

Week 12 — Advanced Sculpting Algorithms

Implicit–mesh hybrids

Multi-resolution and hierarchical sculpting

Research examples and case studies

Part IV: Integration and Advanced Topics

Week 13 — Advanced Topics and Current Research

Selected advanced and emerging topics

Discussion of recent research in geometric modeling and

sculpting

Project refinement

Week 14 — Final Integration and Presentation

Integration of mathematical, topological, and artistic principles

Final project presentations and critique

Course synthesis

Additional Course Information

Course Materials:

Additional course materials and quizzes will be available on

WebAssign:  https://www.webassign.net/ . Students are responsible for

enrolling in WebAssign. For enrollment instructions, please

visit  https://www.getenrolled.com/?courseKey=tamu98102025 . The

class key for enrollment is  tamu 9810 2025 . To access WebAssign,

each student needs to pay a content subscription fee.

Codes, Presentations, and Old Videos:

Additional materials, including code examples, PowerPoint

presentations, and archived videos, will be available in the class’s

shared Google Drive available in the class

website  https://people.tamu.edu/~ergun/courses/viza657/26spring/ .

These materials are  dynamic and will be updated continuously

throughout the semester . Archived class videos are only for

reference purposes. Therefore,  regular class attendance is

essential .
